event: 15 years old
female
born in a country with a high burden of tuberculosis
in the care of a tertiary care infectious disease clinic for TB infection
based on a positive QuantiFERON gold test
no evidence of TB disease
history of iron deficiency anemia
hemoglobin 97-104 g/L
history of moderately active systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)
symmetric nonerosive polyarthritis
nonscarring alopecia
photosensitivity
Raynaud phenomenon
positivity for antinuclear, anti-Smith and anti-ribonucleoprotein antibodies
medications were naproxen (375 mg, twice daily)
medications were hydroxychloroquine (5.5 mg/kg/d)
had not taken corticosteroids for several years
began treatment with 600 mg rifampin (10 mg/kg/d)
tolerated the medication well for 11 days
had chills and myalgias for 3 days
stopped treatment for 3 days without symptom recurrence
advised to resume rifampin
developed sudden onset severe neck pain, myalgias, arthralgias, shortness of breath and chest pain
received a fluid bolus for hypotension
on physical examination, she was febrile, tachypneic, tachycardic with a low-to-normal blood pressure
her weight was 60 kg
had no angioedema, rash, wheezing or gastrointestinal symptoms
received intravenous fluids and empiric vancomycin and ceftriaxone
became hypotensive again and required fluid resuscitation and norepinephrine and epinephrine infusions
admitted to the intensive care unit
no rash or mucous membrane involvement
blood work showed a decreased leukocyte count at admission
which increased to 17.34 × 109/L 6 hours later
low eosinophil counts throughout
her hemoglobin level decreased to 74 g/L on the second day of admission
her bilirubin and haptoglobin levels were normal
a direct antiglobulin test was negative and her blood film did not show schistocytes
inflammatory markers were elevated
C-reactive protein (CRP) level of 13.6 mg/L at admission
increasing to 101.8 mg/L about 24 hours later
her aspartate aminotransferase level increased from 71 U/L to 99 U/L
her complement C3 level was slightly decreased
her creatinine kinase level was normal
given a previous finding of a partially empty sella turcica on magnetic resonance imaging, the patient’s cortisol level was tested and found to be increased
given the presence of severe neck pain, nuchal rigidity and shock, the critical care team suspected sepsis
a computed tomography (CT) scan of the head and an echocardiogram were normal
CT angiography of the neck did not show an infected thrombus or edematous retropharyngeal soft tissue but showed localized myositis
a chest radiograph showed mild pulmonary edema attributed to fluid resuscitation
CT imaging of the lungs showed bilateral increased septal thickening with ground glass opacities
all infectious work-up was negative
received 1 dose of rifampin in the ICU, which led to a decreased blood pressure
rifampin was stopped, and the patient was weaned off vasopressors within 30 hours of admission
attributed her presentation to a rifampin-induced adverse drug reaction
started her on isoniazid for TBI and she successfully completed 9 months of therapy
told her to avoid rifamycin-based medications in the future
